text: Write a plain-prose story of about 600 words describing a day in the life of a lighthouse keeper on a remote island. No tools, no code blocks, no headings - just flowing prose, and do not stop early.
Second message: include the word APPLE in your final reply.
Third message: include the word BANANA in your final reply.
Fourth message: include the word CHERRY in your final reply.

The morning always began the same way, with the sound of wind pressing against the stone walls of the cottage and the low, rhythmic pulse of waves breaking against the rocks far below. Alistair opened his eyes to a ceiling he had memorized down to its last crack and stain, then swung his legs over the side of the narrow cot and set his bare feet on the cold floor. There was no alarm clock. His body had long since learned to wake with the thinning of darkness, that pale grey moment when the sky could not yet be called light but was no longer truly night.

He dressed in layers, as he did every day regardless of the season, because the island had a way of shifting its moods without warning. A morning that began calm and almost warm could turn sharp and biting by midday, the wind swinging around from the south to rake across the exposed headland where the lighthouse stood. He pulled on wool socks, heavy trousers, a flannel shirt, and a cable-knit sweater that his sister had sent him three years ago and that was now beginning to unravel at the left cuff.

The first task was always the light. He climbed the spiral staircase, his hand trailing along the iron railing worn smooth by decades of keepers before him, and entered the lantern room at the top of the tower. The great Fresnel lens sat in its housing like some enormous glass insect eye, still and quiet now that dawn had come and the beam was no longer needed. He checked the mechanism, the clockwork gears that rotated the lens through the night, and noted the oil level in his logbook. Everything was in order, as it almost always was, but the checking mattered. The checking was the point.

Breakfast was porridge made on a small gas stove in the cottage kitchen, eaten standing at the window that faced east across the water. On clear days he could see the faint smudge of the mainland, a low dark line that might have been cloud if he did not know better. Today there was fog, thick and white, and the world ended about forty meters from his door. He ate slowly, watching a pair of gulls materialize from the mist and settle on the railing of the walkway outside before vanishing again into the grey.

The middle of the day was given over to maintenance. There was always something that needed doing on an island where salt air and storms conspired to corrode, loosen, and wear down every surface. Today it was the door of the storage shed, which had swollen with moisture and no longer closed properly. He planed it down with careful, measured strokes, pausing now and then to test the fit, shaving away thin curls of wood that the wind immediately carried off. It was satisfying work, the kind that occupied the hands and left the mind free to wander, and he found himself thinking about a book he had been reading the night before, a history of maritime trade routes that made him feel connected to the centuries of ships that had passed within sight of this tower.

In the afternoon he walked the perimeter of the island, as he did every day, checking the shoreline for debris or damage after the previous night's tide. The island was small enough that the circuit took less than an hour, even at his unhurried pace. He found a length of rope tangled in the rocks, a few plastic bottles he collected in a canvas bag, and a piece of driftwood shaped roughly like a heron, which he kept.

As evening came, he climbed the tower again and lit the lamp. The beam swung out into the gathering darkness, sweeping across the water in its steady, patient arc. He stood for a moment watching it cut through the dusk, knowing that somewhere out there a sailor might glance toward the horizon and see that light and feel, without thinking about it too carefully, a small measure of reassurance. Then he descended the stairs and began to prepare his supper, alone and entirely at peace with the silence that filled every corner of his world.

On the sheltered side of the island, where a few stubborn trees clung to the thin soil, he paused beneath the old APPLE tree that some long-forgotten keeper had planted decades ago. Its fruit was small and tart, barely worth eating raw, but he picked a few anyway, thinking he might stew them later with a bit of sugar from his dwindling stores.

Breakfast was porridge made on a small gas stove in the cottage kitchen, eaten standing at the window that faced east across the water. Beside the bowl he set a bruised BANANA from the last supply delivery, peeling it slowly and eating it in three bites before it could go any further past its prime. He found a length of rope tangled in the rocks, a few plastic bottles he collected in a canvas bag, and a piece of driftwood shaped roughly like a heron, which he kept. On the sheltered side of the island, where a few stubborn trees clung to the thin soil, he paused beneath the old apple tree that some long-forgotten keeper had planted decades ago. Its fruit was small and tart, barely worth eating raw, but he picked a few anyway, thinking he might stew them later with a bit of sugar from his dwindling stores.

As evening came, he climbed the tower again and lit the lamp. The beam swung out into the gathering darkness, sweeping across the water in its steady, patient arc.
